title: Space Hulk is a Forever Game (Review)
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O1awyI-zPJs

A friend deep in the 40K novels wanted to try the tabletop, so Daniel (@dungeondive) finally got Space Hulk back to the table — and was reminded why it stays in his collection despite rarely being played. This is Aliens on your table: one player commands the bulky, lumbering Terminator marines, the other the fast, spider-like gene stealers swarming the corridors, with secret blips and the marines' hidden command points adding bluff and reaction to a brutally simple system.

He lays out five reasons it endures. The production values — chunky embossed tiles, a superb premium rulebook, minis whose facing genuinely matters — are the best he owns. The straightforward rules get you straight to tactics, with OverWatch (free reaction shots) creating constant dread. The 2½-minute turn timer simulates marines reacting to a faster foe and drives nonstop, fast-paced, trash-talking action. And missions stay short enough to never overstay their welcome — unlike, he jokes, Blood Bowl. Drawbacks: large maps take real time to build between missions and can sprawl off a small table. He also recommends The Walking Dead: Here's Negan as the co-op/solo route to the same tension.

----

Daniel keeps Space Hulk forever despite barely playing it, purely for what it is when it does hit the table. Which game earns its shelf space on rare-but-unforgettable plays alone?
